macro_segments.title: The Beginning of Aveilus: Family Unity and the Role of the Gadol Habayis

micro_segments.title: The principle of family aveilus and integration with the shiva observance
Arrival from a nearby vs. faraway place within the first three days
The role of the gadol habayis in defining family unity during shiva
The case of the gadol habayis accompanying the meis to the cemetery
Reconciling the rulings: whether arrival within three days suffices despite absent gadol habayis

macro_segments.title: When Aveilus Begins: The Moment of Transition from Aninus to Mourning

micro_segments.title: The ruling of Rav: joining within three days counts with the family; after three days counts separately
Rava's instruction: aveilus begins when departing from the bier at the city gate
The philosophical question: does aveilus require burial or only the end of aninus

macro_segments.title: The Beginning of Aveilus: Family Unity and the Role of the Gadol Habayis (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's position: joining on day seven is considered part of family shiva

macro_segments.title: When Aveilus Begins: The Moment of Transition from Aninus to Mourning (continued)

micro_segments.title: Joining on day seven: the condition of menachamim still being present
Unresolved question: joining when people are stirring to depart from shiva

macro_segments.title: Scholarly Transmission and Ruling: The Debate over Rabbi Shimon's Position on Day Seven Arrival

micro_segments.title: Transmission of traditions between Rabbi Abba bar Chiya and Rabbi Zeira
Verification of the ruling: the scholar goes to Eretz Yisrael to confirm the teaching
Clarification: we do not rule like Rabban Gamliel regarding treifos but do rule like Rabbi Shimon for aveilus
Dispute between Rav Chisda and Rav Nachman: whether day seven arrival is permitted

macro_segments.title: Distinctions in Aveilus Practices: All Deceased versus Parents

micro_segments.title: Quick burial is praised for all others; for parents it is not praiseworthy
Limitations on business dealings: discretionary for others, required for parents
Uncovering the shoulder: optional for all others, required for parents
Story of two gedolim and the decision not to perform chalitza to avoid imitation
Haircut practices: general rules versus appearance standards
Entry into festive gatherings: thirty days for others, twelve months for parents

macro_segments.title: Joyous Gatherings During Aveilus: Simcha Versus Simchat Merayot

micro_segments.title: Rabba bar bar Chana's distinction: simchat merayot is allowed sooner than full simcha
Baraita challenge: both simcha and merayot are thirty days
Resolution: distinction between initiating a gathering versus reciprocating an obligation
Practical understanding: simcha and merayot treated equally except for reciprocation cases

macro_segments.title: The Practice of Keriah (Rending the Garment): Scope and Method

micro_segments.title: All deceased: tear one tefach; parents: tear to expose the heart
All deceased: outer garment only; parents: all garments must be torn
All deceased: optional hem-to-hem tearing; parents: must tear from hem dividing garment in half
Rabbi Yehuda's view: all keriah must be from hem and look like two halves
All deceased: pinning after seven days, sewing after thirty; parents: pinning after thirty only, never full sewing
Women's consideration: may immediately repin the inner garment by reversing it to preserve modesty
Hand versus instrument: all may use scissors or hands; parents must use hands only
Inside versus outside: all may tear inside; parents tear outside for public demonstration

macro_segments.title: Keriah for Leadership Figures: The Nasi and Chacham

micro_segments.title: Rav Chisda: keriah practices for a nasi are similar to those for parents
Public demonstration of keriah: standing on a platform to show the community
Keriah for a sage: right shoulder; for av beit din: left shoulder; for nasi: both sides

macro_segments.title: Communal Mourning for the Nasi: Cessation of Learning and Public Observance

micro_segments.title: Sage dies: his yeshiva ceases; av beit din dies: all study halls in the city cease
Nasi dies: all study halls in the country cease and people gather in the synagogue
Seven aliyot are read from the Torah while sitting in mourning
People davening separately in their homes while reading Torah communally
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha: community should sit silently, not walk about in the marketplace

topical_tags.term: Three days as ikar aveilus (primary mourning)
Rabbi Shimon (position on day seven arrival)
Halacha Ki-Meikel (ruling according to lenient opinion)
Mavdil (rend into two halves from hem)
Mipnim vs. Mibachutz (inside vs. outside tearing)
